title: Tool
name: NetScan
desc: a small c program that scans your network for devices and open ports. it also displays hostname(if found) vendor and more.

aboutParagraphs: NetScan is a command-line network scanner built from scratch in C. Given a subnet, or run with no arguments to auto-detect the local one, it ping-sweeps the range to find live hosts and tries to put a name to each one through DNS, mDNS, NetBIOS, and SSDP/UPnP lookups, falling back to a MAC vendor lookup when nothing else resolves. It can also deep-scan a single host directly with the --host flag.

Port scanning is done with a multi-threaded TCP connect-scan, with lightweight banner grabbing to help identify what's actually running on each open port. Scan range and connect timeout are both configurable from the command line, and results can be exported to JSON or CSV for use elsewhere.

A diff mode compares the current scan against the last one saved, flagging any devices that are new or have gone missing since. Output is columnar and color-coded in the terminal by default, with flags to hide the IP column or disable color entirely for cleaner piping into other tools. If optional dependencies like avahi, samba, or curl aren't installed, it detects that and prints what's missing along with the install command, rather than just failing name resolution silently.
I built it to get hands-on with raw sockets, threading, and Linux networking internals in C. There's still plenty I'd like to add, but it's a solid, working tool as it stands.

title: tool
name: Localhost Port Scanner (portscan)
desc: A C command-line tool that scans a given port range on localhost and identifies which process is listening on each port.

aboutParagraphs: This project is a small, dependency-free CLI tool for a problem every developer runs into: figuring out what's actually bound to a given port on your own machine, without reaching for netstat or lsof. It's written in C and reads directly from the /proc filesystem, needing no root privileges and no external libraries.

The core approach works in three stages. First, it parses /proc/net/tcp and /proc/net/tcp6, the kernel-exposed tables of every TCP socket on the system, filtering down to sockets in the LISTEN state and recording their port number and socket inode. Second, it builds a full map from socket inode to owning PID by walking every process's /proc/<pid>/fd/ directory, since each open file descriptor is a symlink, and a socket descriptor's symlink target looks like socket:[12345], where 12345 is the inode.

Matching that against the listening sockets found in the first stage reveals which process owns which port. Third, for each resolved PID, it reads /proc/<pid>/comm and /proc/<pid>/cmdline to get a human-readable process name and full command line.

A notable bug during development was in the /proc/net/tcp parsing: the kernel's own header line lists 12 column names, but the actual data rows have fewer whitespace-separated tokens, since fields like tx_queue:rx_queue are colon-joined into a single token. This meant the inode field was being read one column too far to the right until the field-skip count was corrected.

The tool also supports a watch mode (-w), which takes an initial snapshot, then polls on an interval and diffs each new scan against the last one, printing only what changed: a port newly opened, a port that closed, or a port whose owning process changed, such as after a dev server restart. Building this exposed two further bugs: the result arrays were large enough per-entry (due to fixed-size name and command-line buffers)

that stack-allocating them at their maximum size overflowed the default stack and segfaulted, which was fixed by moving them to the heap; and stdout's default full-buffering meant watch mode's output appeared to vanish when the process was killed before its buffer flushed, fixed by switching stdout to line-buffered mode.

The finished tool resolves ports to real process names and command lines correctly both as an unprivileged user and under sudo, with root-owned system services like systemd-resolved only resolving when run with elevated privileges, exactly as expected given standard Linux file permissions.

title: tool
name: Raspberry Pi Wi-Fi to Ethernet Router
desc: Configured a Raspberry Pi 3B as a Wi-Fi-to-Ethernet router using NetworkManager, dnsmasq, and nftables.

aboutParagraphs: This project turns a Raspberry Pi 3B into a functional router that bridges a Wi-Fi internet connection to a wired Ethernet network. Devices plugged into the Pi, directly or through a switch, receive DHCP-assigned IPs and route their traffic out through the Pi's Wi-Fi interface, with no dependency on the host network's own router.

The stack behind it: Raspberry Pi OS Lite (32-bit) as a minimal base with no desktop overhead, NetworkManager (nmcli) for interface configuration since it replaces the legacy dhcpcd approach used in older Pi OS releases, dnsmasq as the DHCP server for Ethernet-side clients, nftables for NAT and firewalling, sysctl for kernel-level IP forwarding, and Tailscale, which was already running on the Pi and had to be accounted for since it also manages its own nftables rules.

The network itself uses the Pi's Ethernet interface as a static gateway at 10.10.10.1/24, with dnsmasq handing out addresses in the 10.10.10.100 to 10.10.10.200 range and pointing clients at 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8 for DNS. Traffic from eth0 is masqueraded out through wlan0.

Most of the actual troubleshooting came from working against a current Raspberry Pi OS install rather than older guides. IP forwarding wasn't where expected, since /etc/sysctl.conf was empty and current Pi OS instead expects drop-in files under /etc/sysctl.d/; this was fixed by adding net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 in a new 99-ipforward.conf and applying it with sysctl --system. There was no dhcpcd.service to restart, since Bookworm replaced dhcpcd with NetworkManager entirely, so all interface configuration went through nmcli connection modify instead. The static IP on eth0 was set the same way, through NetworkManager, and verified with ip addr.

dnsmasq initially failed to start with an "unknown interface eth0" error, because it was starting before the Ethernet interface was available and was bound too strictly; switching its configuration from bind-interfaces to bind-dynamic let it wait for the interface instead of failing outright. On the client side, a laptop didn't reconnect automatically after a cable was unplugged and replugged, since NetworkManager wasn't re-activating the profile on its own; this was fixed manually with nmcli connection up, and permanently by setting connection.autoconnect yes. That same laptop also didn't have dhclient available, which is expected on modern NetworkManager-based distros that don't ship the ISC DHCP client by default, so nmcli connection down and up were used instead.

Tailscale also logged warnings that its NAT table was managed separately through iptables-nft, which turned out to be harmless. The custom masquerade rule for wlan0 was confirmed still active alongside it.

Everything was verified with ip addr and ip route for interfaces and routing, /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward for forwarding status, systemctl status dnsmasq and nft list ruleset for the DHCP and NAT setup, and /var/lib/misc/dnsmasq.leases for issued leases. In the end, the laptop received an IP from the Pi, used it as its default gateway, and kept full internet access with its own Wi-Fi turned off, confirming the Pi was correctly routing all traffic.

title: Tool Website
name: Moduloro
desc: a simple website to host tools on, it loads js from '/tools' and loads them in the sidebar. when clickecked render(container) is ran and your page will load.

aboutParagraphs:
Moduloro started as a simple question: what's the minimum architecture needed to keep adding small tools to a site without the overhead growing with it? The answer was a plugin-style system built on plain ES modules — no framework, no bundler, no build step.
Each tool is a single .js file that exports one function, render(container). An Express server scans the tools/ directory on startup, reads an optional name comment from the top of each file, and generates the sidebar manifest automatically — so adding a new feature is just "create a file, restart the server." No routing config, no registration step, no rebuild.
The frontend loads tools on demand with dynamic import(), passing each one a fresh DOM container to own outright. That constraint — a tool gets a <div> and nothing else — kept every module self-contained and forced consistent patterns for state and re-rendering (each tool keeps its own state in closure and calls a local draw() function to re-render, rather than reaching for a framework's diffing).
The Kanban board was the most involved piece: drag-and-drop columns, inline search that had to survive re-renders without losing input focus, and a detail modal (title, due date, description, timestamp) built without any UI library — just DOM events, contenteditable/form inputs, and manual focus management.
Styling went through a deliberate refactor pass too: rather than scattering hex values and pixel numbers through the CSS, I pulled everything — color, spacing, radius, shadows, transitions — into CSS custom properties in :root, so the whole dark theme can be retuned from one place without touching component rules.
Everything persists client-side via localStorage; there's no backend database, keeping the whole thing a static-file-adjacent app with just enough server logic to make development friction-free.
